headline: Hochul Proposed Banning ICE Collaboration Contracts. Here’s What You Need to Know.
summary: A New York bill intended to change local law enforcement collaboration with ICE would not affect the Village of Allegany's police department, according to its chief, suggesting state-level immigration enforcement reforms may have limited impact on actual local practices.

headline: Hundreds march in Madison, Milwaukee to protest Trump immigration enforcement
summary: Hundreds of protesters in Madison and Milwaukee participated in a national day of action against federal immigration enforcement, marching to the Wisconsin State Capitol and through downtown Milwaukee to oppose ICE operations following the killings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good by federal agents in Minneapolis. Demonstrators called for defunding and abolishing ICE, chanting slogans like "ICE out" and "no ICE, no KKK, no Fascist USA," while speakers including Madison Area Democratic Socialists of America co-chair Halsey Hazzard demanded the Trump administration strip ICE of funding for enforcement and detention. The protests, organized by student groups and immigrant rights advocates, prompted dozens of Madison businesses to close or donate proceeds to advocacy groups, and drew attention to the recent detainment of 5-year-old Liam Ramos in Minnesota, which Vice President J.D. Vance defended. Republican congressional candidate Tom Tiffany responded to the protests by stating he would work with law enforcement to "remove criminal illegal aliens" and urged Minnesota leaders to cooperate with federal agents. No violence or conflicts were reported at either demonstration, though protesters expressed opposition to what they termed the "occupation" of Minneapolis and other cities by federal immigration authorities.

headline: Legislative Notebook: Plans for budget cuts show K-12 education, prison staff would be hit hard
summary: Idaho faces a multi-million dollar budget crunch, prompting the Legislature to request 2% budget cuts from agencies previously considered protected, which would reduce K-12 funding by $55 million and Medicaid by $19.9 million, while forcing furloughs of all state police troopers and over 1,200 prison staff, reducing Capitol security and potentially eliminating the State Police SWAT team, following $450 million in tax cuts approved in 2025 and $4 billion in reduced revenue from prior income tax cuts, with Superintendent Debbie Critchfield refusing to recommend further K-12 cuts, stating the budget represents students, classrooms, teachers and communities.

headline: Additional new budget cuts would force state to furlough Idaho prison guards, state police
summary: Idaho’s budget crisis requires an additional 2% cut (totaling 5% with prior reductions) that would furlough all state police troopers and 1,200+ prison staff, eliminate the ISP SWAT team, and trigger deep K-12 and higher education program cuts; the shortfall stems from $4 billion in recent tax cuts and $155 million in new federal compliance costs, creating political conflict as Governor Little opposes the cuts while Republican legislative leaders insist all agencies must “play in the sandbox” to balance the budget, and Democrats urge repealing $50 million in private school vouchers instead.

headline: Five measles cases reported in southwest Idaho’s Canyon County
summary: Southwest District Health directly notified families potentially exposed to measles at Vallivue schools rather than issuing a public release, citing post-COVID policy concerns about fear mongering, after five unvaccinated Canyon County residents contracted the disease following out-of-state travel. Idaho maintains the nation's lowest kindergarten measles vaccination rate at approximately 80%, well below the 95% herd immunity threshold required to prevent outbreaks. Health officials classified the cases as "probable" based on symptoms without laboratory confirmation and have alerted local healthcare providers. Two children attended school while infectious, though officials report the family voluntarily quarantined and no further community spread has occurred. The health district identifies low vaccination coverage as the primary public health risk, despite the affected schools reportedly meeting the 95% threshold.

headline: Hundreds of Texas public school students walk out to protest ICE killings
summary: On Jan. 30, 2026, Memorial High students in San Antonio walked out to protest ICE. Edgewood ISD Police Department officers and school staff blocked public sidewalks and roads, claiming extended school property rights for student safety. This government action restricted access to public areas during the demonstration. Students were directed back inside after a brief protest. The incident demonstrates law enforcement limiting student activism under safety jurisdiction claims.

headline: Texas Education Agency moves to correct 4,200 errors in Bible-infused curriculum
summary: Marina Hernandez assists pre-K students at Gingerbread Kids Academy in Richmond, demonstrating early childhood education practices that shape K-12 readiness and relate to Texas policy discussions on school choice, teacher workforce development, and expanding early learning programs.

headline: Bird flu hits farm in Caroline County, marking the first case detected in Maryland this year
summary: Maryland officials confirmed highly pathogenic H5 avian influenza at a Caroline County commercial farm, prompting immediate quarantine and depopulation of 37,000 birds. State veterinarian Jennifer Trout assessed low public health risk but warned the virus could evolve to more easily infect humans, citing CDC data of 71 human cases and two fatalities since 2024. The state mandated biosafety protocols for all bird handlers, including protective clothing and equipment disinfection.

headline: Crowd gathers at Denver park to protest ICE during national general strike
summary: Over 1,000 Denver students walked out of school Friday to join a national "ICE Out" protest at La Alma-Lincoln Park against Trump administration immigration enforcement, prompting Denver Public Schools to delay six campuses and Aurora and Adams County districts to close all schools due to staff absences. Dozens of local businesses closed in solidarity as demonstrators condemned fatal ICE shootings in Minneapolis and administration falsehoods about the victims. State Sen. Julie Gonzales, a U.S. Senate candidate, called for ICE abolition at the rally, while U.S. Sens. John Hickenlooper and Michael Bennet demanded congressional reforms including body camera requirements and a ban on masked agents in forthcoming DHS funding legislation. The protest reflected growing Colorado political pressure to overhaul federal immigration operations.

headline: Mayors describe ICE presence in their cities
summary: U.S. mayors from both parties convened in Washington, D.C. to address escalating federal immigration enforcement under the Trump administration, expressing bipartisan concern over threats to revoke funding and prosecute sanctuary cities. Mayors from Minnesota, Colorado, and California reported ICE operations causing community fear, reduced 911 calls, and disruptions to construction and healthcare sectors, while criticizing agents' lack of city-appropriate training. Several mayors, drawing on personal immigrant and Japanese internment histories, urged local coordination with faith and legal leaders to protect constituents amid federal pressure.

headline: Thousands protest ICE in Albuquerque’s center
summary: On January 30, 2026, thousands of New Mexicans protested at Albuquerque's Civic Plaza and statewide against federal immigration crackdowns, joining a National Shutdown that prompted City Hall to close early and numerous businesses to shutter in solidarity. Demonstrators marched to the Dennis Chavez Federal Building demanding ICE abolition following the killings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good by immigration agents and reports of 30 deaths in ICE custody last year. Organizers from the Southwest Solidarity Network condemned Congressional inaction and called for systemic change rather than incremental reform. Participants—including veterans, nurses, and children of immigrants—framed their resistance as a necessary response to Trump Administration policies. The protest reflected broader political unrest over federal immigration enforcement and its local impact.

headline: NM Legislature day 11 recap: ICE protests, medical malpractice, Epstein subcommittee
summary: Lawmakers debated House Bill 9 on Jan. 30, 2026, which would prohibit local governments from signing ICE detention contracts and, under a governor-supported amendment, prevent local law enforcement from transferring jail inmates to ICE custody. Santa Fe high schoolers protested outside the Roundhouse in support of the ban while a counter-protester called for "deporting more liberal teenagers." Rep. John Block (R-Alamogordo) introduced a floor amendment allowing counties to seek state reimbursement if detention centers close in response to the legislation. Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham endorsed the expanded prohibitions on ICE cooperation. At publication time, the House had not yet voted on the bill.

headline: Community rallies for longtime Somali taxi driver detained by ICE
summary: ICE detained Somali refugee and legal permanent resident Hussien Noor Hussien on New Year's Day at Burlington airport, despite his compliance with post-conviction supervision terms and a pending 2027 immigration court date. The detention occurs amid the Trump administration's escalating crackdown on Somali immigrants, including policy actions like freezing Minnesota child care funding. Community organizations and Vermont Law School's Justice Reform Clinic are challenging the detention, citing denial of religious accommodations and concerns about fairness and proportionality. ICE publicized the arrest on social media as a "criminal alien" apprehension. The case exemplifies tensions between federal immigration enforcement and state-level immigrant support systems.

headline: CT officials approve UConn acquisition of Prospect-owned hospital
summary: The Connecticut Office of Health Strategy approved UConn Health's $13 million acquisition of Waterbury Hospital from bankrupt Prospect Medical Holdings under a new expedited "emergency certificate of need" process, mandating maintenance of all services—including women's health and reproductive care—for three years to prevent closure. Governor Ned Lamont praised the deal, which follows Hartford HealthCare's recent purchase of Prospect's two other Connecticut hospitals. The state committed $390 million in bonding for capital improvements through the UConn 2000 program. This marks the second transaction under the expedited process created by legislators after criticism of delays in prior hospital sales. The approval concludes a turbulent sale process that collapsed when a previous $435 million agreement with Yale New Haven Health fell through amid litigation, a crippling cyberattack, and bankruptcy.

headline: CT police: Cops must intervene if they see excessive force by feds
summary: Connecticut's 2020 police accountability law requiring officers to intervene when witnessing brutality applies to federal immigration agents, according to state officials; the duty to intervene was highlighted following incidents in Minneapolis where ICE agents fatally shot unarmed civilians during immigration-related protests, with state officials criticizing federal agents' lack of de-escalation training and coordination with local police, while noting that protests increasingly target federal crackdowns on undocumented immigration.

headline: Eviction prevention cuts will only drive more costs for WA
summary: Gov. Bob Ferguson's supplemental budget proposal includes a $3 million cut to Washington's Eviction Prevention Program that would deny legal representation to 2,000 renters facing eviction starting July 1, 2026; state legislators must decide whether to enact this cut or fully fund the program, which serves over 33,500 tenants and achieves permanent housing solutions in 90% of cases, while critics argue the cut would ultimately increase state costs in emergency rooms, shelters, and foster care systems.

headline: Illness sidelines chair of key WA budget committee
summary: With Rep. Steve Tharinger absent due to medical issues, Rep. Lisa Callan is leading the House Capital Budget Committee and will be the primary Democrat drafting the 2026 supplemental capital budget, which funds investments in housing, schools, colleges, parks, and natural resources.

headline: ‘Not what our faith teaches’: Nashville faith groups host vigil outside Homeland Security office
summary: Dozens of clergy and community members gathered at Nashville's Department of Homeland Security field office for a vigil protesting nine deaths connected to immigration law enforcement this year, including two individuals shot by federal agents in Minneapolis. Religious leaders criticized the "dangerous rise of fear-driven policies" and "white supremacy and nationalism" in immigration enforcement, directly challenging current government practices. The event highlighted at least six immigrant deaths in ICE custody since January 1 and a fatal shooting by an off-duty ICE agent, demonstrating opposition to federal immigration policies. Faith organizers framed the vigil as a moral imperative to support vulnerable populations and condemn what they described as government actions "steeped in fear and absolutely lacking compassion." The protest occurred despite local hardship from a winter storm that left over 71,000 residents without power, emphasizing the urgency of the issue for participants.

headline: Nessel won’t rule out legal action in case of ICE surge, calls current actions ‘deeply concerning’
summary: Michigan AG Dana Nessel is preparing her office for potential federal immigration enforcement surges while addressing current ICE activity, including Ypsilanti arrests and Willow Run Airport detention flights; she opposes local law enforcement collaboration with federal agents, citing liability risks and resource diversion, and is working with Gov. Whitmer to develop best practices; Nessel encourages legal documentation of federal interactions and would consider lawsuits for rights violations, referencing similar actions in Illinois and Minnesota; she stated her office would investigate crimes by federal agents against Michigan residents despite anticipated non-cooperation; and she condemned U.S. AG Pam Bondi's linking of de-escalation requests to voter registration data demands as "blackmail," joining 21 other state AGs in opposition.

headline: Hundreds take to the streets of Raleigh and Durham to demand ‘ICE out’
summary: On January 30, 2026, hundreds of protesters marched in Raleigh, Durham, and across North Carolina as part of a "national shutdown" movement opposing federal immigration crackdown, with N.C. State students walking out of classes and calls for "no work, no school, no shopping" in solidarity with Minnesota activists killed earlier this month. Organized by left-wing advocacy groups including the Party for Socialism and Liberation and the NC Poor People’s Campaign, demonstrations demanded justice for shooting victims Renee Good and Alex Pretti and highlighted concerns about due process erosion and family targeting under federal immigration policy. Speakers emphasized the political and economic dimensions of the crackdown, with one noting profit motives in detention and others expressing personal fears about ICE actions against family members.

headline: Louisiana leans on hunters to keep ‘zombie deer’ disease in check
summary: The CDC and health experts warn Louisiana residents not to eat meat from deer infected with chronic wasting disease due to concerns about potential human transmission, despite no documented cases. State wildlife officials have declared an emergency, expanding control areas to six parishes. They prohibit deer baiting in "enhanced mitigation zones" and restrict transport of nervous system tissues from carcasses. The surveillance program relies on hunters submitting 4,000 samples annually, with 3,200 collected this season. These measures aim to prevent a potential public health crisis similar to mad cow disease.

headline: Two Kansas men charged with threatening congresswoman from Illinois, ICE agents
summary: Two Kansas men were federally indicted for threatening public officials: Adam Osborn for threatening Rep. Ilhan Omar, a prominent immigration advocate, and Joaquin Hernandez for threatening ICE agents. Osborn faces charges of interstate communications with a threat and retaliating against a federal official. Hernandez faces similar charges for his threats against immigration enforcement officers. U.S. Attorney Ryan Kriegshauser stated that threats of political violence destabilize democratic governance and will not be tolerated. The cases highlight tensions surrounding immigration policy and the security risks faced by both advocates and enforcement officials.

headline: Black men in California face higher risks and higher bills for prostate cancer
summary: California has no prostate cancer screening legislation despite Black men facing the highest diagnosis and death rates; Gov. Newsom vetoed AB 632, which would have eliminated out-of-pocket screening costs for high-risk men, citing premium concerns; public health advocates attribute disparities to social determinants of health and demand policy action.
